title: practical english usage - Part 332
content:
For let introducing first- and third-person imperatives (e.g. tet's go for a walk; Let me see; He was let out of prison early for good behaviour.

This structure is also common in the passive.

Let him  in, could you? Those kids let my tyres down.

You’d better let the dog out of the car.

expressing movement.

Let can be followed by an object and a prepositional phrase or adverb particle 3 with    object     + preposition         / adverb      particle After questioning he was allowed to go home.

This structure is unusual in passive forms; we  prefer allow.

2  not   used    in  passives Don’t let go of Mummy ’s hand.

Let go oʃmeans ‘ stop holding'.

Could you let me have the bill for the car repair?

I’ll let you know my travel dates next week.

Note the expressions let. .. know (= tell, inform) and let... have (= send, give).

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 514 vocabulary •         like: verb For would like with a perfect infinitive (e.g. I would like to have seen that), ►    90.

If you like to come this way...

Would is sometimes dropped in this structure.

If you would like to come this way.. .

If you would like to take a seat, I'll see if Mr Smithers is free.

sometimes dropped.

Polite requests often begin If you would like...; the following clause is 'How about playing tennis?' 'I'd like to.’ Would like to can be used instead of repeating a whole infinitive (►  280).

NOT   Do you like some more coffee?

Do you like ...? is not used in this way.

'Would you like to dance? 'Yes, OK.' (     Would you like dancing?-...) I'd like two kilos of tomatoes, please.

requests and offers.

We use would like + infinitive as a polite way of saying ‘ want’ , especially in 6  would       like She likes the children to go to bed early during the week.

Like + object + infinitive is possible.

‘ Why didn't you tell me before? 'I didn't like to disturb you at home.' Not like to can mean ‘ think it better not to’ .

When I'm pouring tea I like to put the milk in first.

/ like to do the shopping early on Saturday mornings.

We   can use like + infinitive to talk about choices and habits.

5 like    + infinitive: I don't like people phoning / to phone me in the middle of the night.

Like + object + verb is possible.

I really liked working with him  on his boat last week.

To  talk about enjoying something on one occasion, we  use like .. .ing.

Children always like listening / to listen to stories.

I really like walking / to walk in the woods.

common in British English) or like + infinitive.

To  talk about enjoying activities in general, we  can use like .. .ing (especially enjoyment 4 like    .. .ing: (NOT I  like very m uch you and your sister.) or I like you and your sister very much .  / very much  like you and your sister.

Very much  does not come between like and its object (►    196).

/ very much  like his latest film. (     I very like his latest film.) We   can use very much  with like, but not very alone.

F or exceptions, ►    514.7 below.

'How do you feel about ballet? 'I like it.' (     ...I       .) Like cannot normally be used without an object.

2  not   used    without      an   object vocabulary •  515  like and as: similarity, function He believed, as did all his family, that the king was their supreme lord.

She was a Catholic, as were most of her friends.

the inverted word order, ►    270).

In a very formal style, as is sometimes followed by auxiliary verb + subject (note 4 inverted      word    order:    as   did    all   his    family Nobody loves you like I do.

common in an informal style.

In modern English, like is often used as a conjunction instead of as. This is most 3 like    I do   (informal) On  Friday, as on Tuesday, the meeting will be at 8.30.

In 1939, as in 1914, everybody seemed to want war.

We often drink tea with the meal, as they do in China.

Nobody knows her as I do.

as + preposition phrase as + clause beginning with a preposition.

As is a conjunction. We   use it before a clause, and before an expression 2  as   (similarity):     as   I do In mountainous countries, like Peru,.. .
